question: A 59-year-old woman has had a minimally itchy, slowly enlarging plaque on the dorsum of her left hand for 4 years after a prior skin puncture. She is a long-standing insulin-treated diabetic and topical antibiotic therapy produced no improvement. Based on the clinical presentation and the photograph, what is the most appropriate next step in management?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the photograph and note the lesion's morphology. On the dorsum of the hand there is a solitary, well-demarcated plaque with a central crusted, scabbed area that looks indurated and slightly irregular. The surrounding skin shows erythema with a darker hyperpigmented rim; the lesion appears chronic rather than an acute, diffuse rash.

Next, I relate these visual findings to the clinical stem: a 4-year history of a slowly enlarging, minimally itchy plaque that began after a puncture and did not improve with topical antibiotic therapy in an insulin-treated diabetic. The long duration, focal induration, central crusting, and failure of topical antibiotics together suggest this is not a straightforward acute bacterial infection that would reliably respond to more topical measures.

Then, I consider how each management option aligns with these observations. A biopsy (punch/incisional) would provide tissue for definitive diagnosis and guide therapy for a chronic, atypical lesion that has not responded to conservative topical treatment. Empiric oral antibiotics are unlikely to be helpful given prior topical antibiotic failure and the chronic, localized morphology. High-potency topical corticosteroid could mask or worsen an underlying infectious or neoplastic process and is therefore not appropriate without a diagnosis. Cryotherapy is typically used for small benign surface lesions and is not appropriate for a chronic, crusted, indurated plaque. Wide local excision without first obtaining a diagnosis is unnecessarily aggressive.

Therefore, obtaining a punch or incisional skin biopsy is the most appropriate next step to establish a diagnosis and direct further management.
caption: Well-defined erythematous plaque with hyperpigmented borders and overlying crust present on the dorsum of the left hand
sub_caption: Well-defined erythematous plaque with hyperpigmented borders and overlying crust present on the dorsum of the left hand
image_context: A 59-year-old woman presented with a minimally itchy and tender plaque on the dorsum of the left hand since last 4 years. According to the patient it started as a small papule following blood collection at the site and gradually increased in size. She was a known diabetic; controlled on insulin for the past 15 years. She had used a topical antibiotic intermittently with no relief. On clinical examination, there was a well-defined erythematous plaque with hyperpigmented borders and overlying crust [<xref ref-type="fig" rid="IDOJ-6-62-g001">Figure 1</xref>]. Rest of the dermatological and systemic examination of the patient was normal.]. Rest of the dermatological and systemic examination of the patient was normal.
relevant_image_context: A 59-year-old woman presented with a minimally itchy and tender plaque on the dorsum of the left hand since last 4 years. According to the patient it started as a small papule following blood collection at the site and gradually increased in size. She was a known diabetic; controlled on insulin for the past 15 years. She had used a topical antibiotic intermittently with no relief. On clinical examination, there was a well-defined erythematous plaque with hyperpigmented borders and overlying crust. Rest of the dermatological and systemic examination of the patient was normal.
